name: split-panel
description: Featured left panel + supporting list on the right.
purpose: Use when one prominent statement deserves a dark sidebar and the right side carries the substantiating points.

panel-heading.description: Heading shown in the dark left panel.

panel-eyebrow.description: Optional rubric label below the panel heading.

points.description: Right-side supporting points. Lead each with **Label.** then body text.

skeleton:
<!-- _class: split-panel -->

## Panel headline that frames the side points.

### Section rubric

- **First point.** Supporting sentence explaining the first point.
- **Second point.** Supporting sentence explaining the second point.
- **Third point.** Supporting sentence explaining the third point.
sample:
<!-- _class: split-panel -->

## A featured statement on the left lands the argument.

### Use when

- **One claim deserves emphasis.** The panel headline carries the weight; the right side substantiates.
- **You need both a thesis and proof.** Statement on the left, evidence list on the right.
- **The right side is short.** Three to four supporting points; longer lists belong in `cards-stack`.
- **The mirror modifier flips it.** Image-style decks often want the panel on the right.

whenToUse.title: Thesis plus proof
whenToUse.body: One bold claim deserves dedicated visual weight on the left; supporting points sit to the right. Use when the audience needs to hear the claim before the evidence.

whenToUse.title: Section openers with substance
whenToUse.body: More substantive than a divider, but still anchored by a single statement. Three to four supporting points keep the panel from feeling cluttered.

whenToUse.title: Mirror for image-heavy decks
whenToUse.body: The `mirror` variant flips the panel to the right. Useful when the deck's visual rhythm wants accents on alternating sides.

antiPatterns.title: Longer than four points
antiPatterns.body: If the right side runs past four points, the panel and list lose balance. Move to `cards-stack` for longer lists or split into two slides.

antiPatterns.title: No panel headline
antiPatterns.body: The dark panel exists to carry a statement. An empty or generic heading wastes the strongest visual real estate on the slide.

antiPatterns.title: Stacking many split-panel slides in a row
antiPatterns.body: The dark sidebar is a strong visual signal. Three split-panel slides back-to-back numbs the audience to it. Intersperse with content or stats.

related.name: split-statement
related.when: thesis + one big-number — quantitative version of split-panel

related.name: big-number
related.when: single statement, no supporting list — the full-canvas version

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: the right-side list grows past four points

mirror.label: Mirror — panel on the right
mirror.caption: Flips the dark accent panel from the left to the right. Use when the deck's visual rhythm wants alternating accent sides, or when the right-to-left reading flow suits the claim.
mirror.sample:
<!-- _class: split-panel mirror -->

## And the same claim, now with the panel on the right.

### Use when

- **Decks with image slides.** Image bleed sits left; the panel reads as a closing statement on the right.
- **Alternating visual rhythm.** Pair a default split-panel and a mirrored one across a section to keep the eye moving.
- **Right-to-left framing.** The argument reads more naturally when the claim follows the evidence rather than preceding it.
